meta.purpose: PhD/Dr-level evidence base for all life coaching domains
meta.usage: Shared across all personas - they reference this, apply through their methodology

cbt.name: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy
cbt.coreIdea: Thoughts influence emotions and behaviors; changing thought patterns changes outcomes
cbt.keyPrinciple: The cognitive model: Situation → Thought → Emotion → Behavior
cbt.efficacy: Over 2,000 studies; gold standard for anxiety, depression, anger

techniques.name: Behavioral Experiments
techniques.example: If you believe 'setting boundaries will ruin relationships,' test with small boundary

cognitiveDistortions.name: All-or-Nothing Thinking
cognitiveDistortions.description: Seeing things in black and white
cognitiveDistortions.example: If I'm not perfect, I'm a failure

cognitiveDistortions.name: Catastrophizing
cognitiveDistortions.example: If I say no, they'll hate me forever

cognitiveDistortions.name: Mind Reading
cognitiveDistortions.description: Assuming you know what others think

cognitiveDistortions.name: Should Statements
cognitiveDistortions.description: Rigid rules about how things must be

cognitiveDistortions.name: Emotional Reasoning
cognitiveDistortions.example: I feel guilty, so I must be wrong

cognitiveDistortions.name: Personalization
cognitiveDistortions.example: Their bad mood is because of me

cognitiveDistortions.name: Overgeneralization
cognitiveDistortions.description: Drawing broad conclusions from single events
cognitiveDistortions.example: I failed once, I'll always fail

cognitiveDistortions.name: Magnification/Minimization
cognitiveDistortions.example: My mistake was huge, my win was luck

citations: Beck, A.T. (1976). Cognitive Therapy and the Emotional Disorders
Beck, J.S. (2011). Cognitive Behavior Therapy: Basics and Beyond (2nd ed.)
Hofmann, S.G. et al. (2012). The Efficacy of Cognitive Behavioral Therapy: A Review of Meta-analyses

act.name: Acceptance and Commitment Therapy
act.coreIdea: Psychological flexibility through acceptance, mindfulness, and values-based action
act.keyPrinciple: It's not about eliminating difficult feelings, but changing relationship with them
act.efficacy: Strong evidence for anxiety, depression, chronic pain, substance abuse

hexaflex.description: Allowing thoughts and feelings without struggle
Seeing thoughts as thoughts, not facts
Flexible attention to the here and now
The observing self, not defined by content

techniques.name: Defusion Exercises
examples: Say the thought slowly, then fast, then in a silly voice
Prefix thoughts with 'I'm having the thought that...'
Visualize thoughts as leaves floating on a stream

techniques.name: Values Card Sort
techniques.description: Ranking life domains by importance to clarify priorities

citations: Hayes, S.C. (2004). Get Out of Your Mind and Into Your Life
Harris, R. (2008). The Happiness Trap
A-Tjak, J.G.L. et al. (2015). A meta-analysis of ACT for clinically relevant mental and physical health problems

dbt.name: Dialectical Behavior Therapy
dbt.coreIdea: Balance acceptance and change through skills training
dbt.keyPrinciple: Dialectics - two seemingly opposite things can be true simultaneously
dbt.efficacy: Gold standard for BPD, emotion dysregulation, self-harm

crisisSkills: TIPP (Temperature, Intense exercise, Paced breathing, Paired muscle relaxation)

skills: ABC PLEASE (Accumulate positives, Build mastery, Cope ahead, treat PhysicaL illness, balance Eating, avoid mood-Altering substances, balance Sleep, get Exercise)
DEAR MAN (Describe, Express, Assert, Reinforce, Mindful, Appear confident, Negotiate)
FAST (Fair, no Apologies, Stick to values, Truthful)

citations: Linehan, M.M. (2014). DBT Skills Training Manual (2nd ed.)
Linehan, M.M. (1993). Cognitive-Behavioral Treatment of Borderline Personality Disorder

ifs.name: Internal Family Systems
ifs.coreIdea: The mind is naturally multiple; we have parts that carry different roles
ifs.keyPrinciple: All parts have positive intent; healing comes from Self-leadership
ifs.efficacy: Growing evidence base; NREPP listed; effective for trauma, depression

partTypes.role: Reactive protectors that numb or distract
Wounded parts carrying pain from the past

citations: Schwartz, R.C. (2021). No Bad Parts
Schwartz, R.C. & Sweezy, M. (2019). Internal Family Systems Therapy (2nd ed.)

polyvagal.name: Polyvagal Theory
polyvagal.coreIdea: The autonomic nervous system has three states that influence behavior and emotion
polyvagal.keyPrinciple: Safety is a biological imperative; neuroception detects threat before cognition
polyvagal.efficacy: Foundational for trauma treatment; informs SE, DBT, attachment work

states.description: Social engagement system - safe and connected

polyvagal.coregulation: We regulate our nervous systems through connection with others
citations: Porges, S.W. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory
Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy

somaticExperiencing.name: Somatic Experiencing
somaticExperiencing.coreIdea: Trauma is stored in the body; healing requires completing thwarted survival responses
somaticExperiencing.keyPrinciple: Titration - processing small amounts of activation at a time to prevent overwhelm
somaticExperiencing.efficacy: Growing evidence; effective for PTSD, chronic pain, anxiety

keyPrinciples.description: Moving between activation and calm states
Small doses of activation to prevent overwhelm
Releasing trapped survival energy through trembling, shaking, breathing
Building capacity through positive body sensations

citations: Levine, P.A. (1997). Waking the Tiger: Healing Trauma
Levine, P.A. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice: How the Body Releases Trauma

motivationalInterviewing.name: Motivational Interviewing
motivationalInterviewing.coreIdea: Ambivalence is normal; change comes from within when resistance is not met with force
motivationalInterviewing.keyPrinciple: Roll with resistance; evoke rather than impose
motivationalInterviewing.efficacy: Over 200 RCTs; effective for addiction, health behavior change

spirit.description: Drawing out rather than putting in

oars.example: What would be different if you could set boundaries?
You've shown real courage in recognizing this pattern
It sounds like part of you wants change, and part feels scared
So you've noticed... and you're wondering...

questions: On a scale of 0-10, how important is this change to you?
Why are you at a [number] and not a 0?
What would it take to move from [number] to [number+1]?

citations: Miller, W.R. & Rollnick, S. (2012). Motivational Interviewing (3rd ed.)
Rollnick, S. et al. (2008). Motivational Interviewing in Health Care

attachmentTheory.name: Attachment Theory
attachmentTheory.coreIdea: Early relationships create internal working models that shape adult relating
attachmentTheory.keyPrinciple: Earned secure attachment is possible through consistent, responsive relationships
attachmentTheory.efficacy: Foundational for developmental psychology; informs EFT, mentalization

attachmentStyles.internalModel: I am worthy; others are available
I am not enough; I need others to feel okay
attachmentStyles.inRelationships: Seeks closeness, fears abandonment, hyperactivates attachment system

attachmentStyles.internalModel: I don't need others; self-reliance is safest
I want closeness but expect hurt

earnedSecurity.description: Moving toward secure attachment through corrective emotional experiences

citations: Bowlby, J. (1988). A Secure Base
Levine, A. & Heller, R. (2010). Attached
Johnson, S.M. (2008). Hold Me Tight

gottmanMethod.name: Gottman Method Couples Therapy
gottmanMethod.coreIdea: Relationship success can be predicted and improved through specific behaviors
gottmanMethod.keyPrinciple: The 'magic ratio' - 5 positive interactions for every 1 negative
gottmanMethod.efficacy: 40+ years of research; 90%+ accuracy predicting divorce

fourHorsemen.antidote: Gentle startup - 'I feel... about... I need...'
Self-soothe, take a break, then return

soundRelationshipHouse: Build Love Maps (know partner's inner world)
citations: Gottman, J.M. & Silver, N. (1999). The Seven Principles for Making Marriage Work
Gottman, J.M. (2011). The Science of Trust

selfCompassion.name: Self-Compassion
selfCompassion.coreIdea: Treating yourself with the same kindness you'd offer a good friend
selfCompassion.keyPrinciple: Self-compassion is not self-pity or self-indulgence; it's acknowledging suffering
selfCompassion.efficacy: Strong evidence for well-being, resilience, reduced anxiety and depression

threeComponents.description: Being warm and understanding toward ourselves
Recognizing suffering is part of shared human experience
Holding painful feelings in balanced awareness

practices.name: Self-Compassion Break
steps: This is a moment of suffering
Suffering is part of being human
May I be kind to myself

practices.name: Compassionate Letter Writing
practices.description: Write to yourself from the perspective of a compassionate friend

practices.name: Self-Compassion Phrase
practices.description: Develop personal mantra for difficult moments

citations: Neff, K. (2011). Self-Compassion: The Proven Power of Being Kind to Yourself
Neff, K. & Germer, C. (2018). The Mindful Self-Compassion Workbook

leadingExperts.name: Dr. Henry Cloud & Dr. John Townsend
leadingExperts.contribution: Boundaries framework - what's mine, what's yours

leadingExperts.name: Dr. Harriet Braiker
leadingExperts.contribution: Disease to Please - people-pleasing as addiction

leadingExperts.name: Nedra Glover Tawwab, LCSW
leadingExperts.contribution: Set Boundaries, Find Peace - practical application

keyFindings: Boundaries are not walls - they're about clarity, not disconnection
Guilt often signals growth, not wrongdoing (Cloud & Townsend)
Clear is kind, unclear is unkind (Brown)
Resentment is a signal that a boundary is needed
citations: Cloud, H. & Townsend, J. (1992). Boundaries: When to Say Yes, How to Say No
Brown, B. (2018). Dare to Lead
Tawwab, N.G. (2021). Set Boundaries, Find Peace

leadingExperts.name: Dr. Raymond Novaco
leadingExperts.contribution: Pioneer of anger management; stress inoculation for anger

leadingExperts.name: Dr. Marsha Linehan
leadingExperts.contribution: DBT emotion regulation and distress tolerance

leadingExperts.name: Dr. Harriet Lerner
leadingExperts.contribution: The Dance of Anger - anger as information

keyFindings: Anger is a secondary emotion - often covering hurt, fear, or shame
Venting increases anger; cognitive reappraisal decreases it
Physiological arousal precedes anger - early intervention key
Anger has adaptive functions - signals boundary violations, injustice
citations: Novaco, R.W. (1975). Anger Control: The Development and Evaluation of an Experimental Treatment
Kassinove, H. & Tafrate, R.C. (2002). Anger Management
Lerner, H. (2014). The Dance of Anger

leadingExperts.name: Dr. Piers Steel
leadingExperts.contribution: Temporal Motivation Theory; The Procrastination Equation

keyFindings: Procrastination is an emotion regulation problem, not a time management problem
The Procrastination Equation: Motivation = (Expectancy × Value) / (Impulsiveness × Delay)
Self-forgiveness for past procrastination reduces future procrastination
Implementation intentions ('when-then' planning) highly effective

procrastinationEquation.expectancy: Belief you can complete the task
procrastinationEquation.delay: How far away the deadline is

interventions: Increase Expectancy: Break into smaller tasks, build self-efficacy
Increase Value: Connect to values, make it more enjoyable, reward progress
Decrease Impulsiveness: Remove distractions, commitment devices
Decrease Delay: Create artificial deadlines, accountability
citations: Steel, P. (2007). The nature of procrastination: A meta-analytic review
Pychyl, T.A. (2013). Solving the Procrastination Puzzle
Sirois, F.M. (2014). Procrastination and Stress

keyFindings: Burnout has three dimensions: exhaustion, cynicism, inefficacy
Burnout is a workplace/systemic issue, not individual weakness
Stress cycle must be completed - emotions need physical resolution
Recovery requires both rest AND active stress completion
citations: Maslach, C. & Leiter, M.P. (2016). Burnout: A Guide to Identifying Burnout
Nagoski, E. & Nagoski, A. (2019). Burnout: The Secret to Unlocking the Stress Cycle

leadingExperts.name: Dr. Paul Hewitt & Dr. Gordon Flett
Brené Brown, PhD
leadingExperts.contribution: Perfectionism as armor; distinction from healthy striving

keyFindings: Perfectionism is increasing generationally, especially socially prescribed
Perfectionism correlates with depression, anxiety, eating disorders, suicide
Perfectionism is not striving for excellence - it's fear-based
Perfectionism is about perception management, not self-improvement

perfectionismTypes.description: Perceiving others demand perfection from you

citations: Hewitt, P.L. & Flett, G.L. (1991). Dimensions of Perfectionism
Curran, T. & Hill, A.P. (2019). Perfectionism Is Increasing Over Time
Brown, B. (2010). The Gifts of Imperfection

leadingExperts.name: Dr. Bessel van der Kolk
leadingExperts.contribution: The Body Keeps the Score; trauma as embodied experience

keyFindings: Trauma is stored in the body, not just the mind
Trauma responses are survival adaptations, not character flaws
Post-traumatic growth is possible - many report positive changes
Safety and stabilization must precede trauma processing

windowOfTolerance.concept: The optimal zone of arousal where we can function and process
windowOfTolerance.hyperarousal: Fight/flight - anxiety, panic, hypervigilance, racing thoughts
windowOfTolerance.intervention: Widen the window through grounding, co-regulation, titrated exposure

citations: van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score
Herman, J. (1992). Trauma and Recovery
Levine, P.A. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice

leadingExperts.name: Dr. Jean Twenge
leadingExperts.contribution: iGen; smartphone impact on youth mental health

keyFindings: Smartphones activate same dopamine pathways as slot machines
Social media use correlates with depression, especially in teens
FOMO (Fear of Missing Out) drives compulsive checking
Digital minimalism improves focus, relationships, wellbeing
digitalMinimalismPrinciples: Clutter is costly - attention is finite
Optimization is important - find right tool for job
Intentionality is satisfying - choose don't default
citations: Alter, A. (2017). Irresistible: The Rise of Addictive Technology
Newport, C. (2019). Digital Minimalism
Twenge, J. (2017). iGen

keyFindings: Diets fail long-term 95%+ of the time; often lead to weight gain
Weight stigma is more harmful to health than weight itself
Intuitive eating improves physical and mental health outcomes
Body dissatisfaction is normative but not inevitable

haesParadigm.core: Health can be pursued at any size through self-care behaviors
principles: Joyful movement over exercise as punishment

citations: Bacon, L. (2010). Health at Every Size
Tribole, E. & Resch, E. (2020). Intuitive Eating (4th ed.)
Tylka, T.L. (2011). Positive psychology perspectives on body image

keyFindings: Bids for connection predict relationship success - turning toward vs. away
Perpetual problems (69%) cannot be solved, only managed with dialogue
Repair attempts matter more than avoiding conflict
Secure functioning requires both partners prioritizing relationship
citations: Gottman, J.M. (2011). The Science of Trust
Johnson, S. (2008). Hold Me Tight
Perel, E. (2017). The State of Affairs

leadingExperts.name: Dr. Russell Barkley
leadingExperts.contribution: ADHD as executive function deficit; evidence-based treatment

leadingExperts.name: Dr. Ned Hallowell
leadingExperts.contribution: Strengths-based ADHD approach; ADHD as 'race car brain'

leadingExperts.name: Dr. Temple Grandin
leadingExperts.contribution: Autism as different, not less; visual thinking

keyFindings: ADHD is not laziness - it's an executive function difference
Autism involves different neurological wiring, not deficit
Environment fit matters more than 'fixing' the individual
Many neurodivergent traits are strengths in right context
citations: Barkley, R.A. (2015). Attention-Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder: A Handbook
Hallowell, E. & Ratey, J. (2011). Driven to Distraction
Grandin, T. (2013). The Autistic Brain

leadingExperts.name: Dr. Emily Nagoski
leadingExperts.contribution: Sexual response cycle; 'Come As You Are'

keyFindings: Desire often follows arousal, not the reverse (responsive vs. spontaneous)
Differentiation (solid sense of self) enables deeper intimacy
Security and novelty are both needed - the erotic paradox
Context matters more than technique for desire

dualControlModel.description: Sexual response has both accelerator (excitation) and brakes (inhibition)
dualControlModel.accelerator: Things that turn you on - touch, context, connection
dualControlModel.brakes: Things that turn you off - stress, body image, relationship issues
dualControlModel.intervention: Often more effective to remove brakes than press accelerator

citations: Perel, E. (2006). Mating in Captivity
Nagoski, E. (2015). Come As You Are
Schnarch, D. (2009). Passionate Marriage

leadingExperts.name: Dr. Helen Fisher
leadingExperts.contribution: Brain on love; rejection as withdrawal

keyFindings: Romantic rejection activates same brain regions as physical pain
Heartbreak follows similar stages to grief (not always linear)
Positive reframing of breakup predicts better recovery
New experiences and self-expansion aid recovery
recoveryFactors: Who initiated the breakup (rejected recovers slower)
citations: Fisher, H. (2016). Anatomy of Love
Winch, G. (2018). How to Fix a Broken Heart
Lewandowski, G.W. (2009). Promoting positive outcomes after relationship dissolution

leadingExperts.name: Carl Jung
leadingExperts.contribution: Individuation; second half of life psychology

leadingExperts.name: Dr. Carlo Strenger
leadingExperts.contribution: Midlife crisis revision; fear of insignificance

keyFindings: Midlife 'crisis' is often midlife 'awakening' or transition
Life satisfaction follows U-curve - dips in midlife, rises after
Generativity (contributing to next generation) key to wellbeing
Jung: First half builds ego, second half finds meaning

jungianIndividuation.concept: Becoming who you truly are by integrating all parts of self

eriksonGenerativity.generativity: Contributing to future generations through parenting, mentoring, creating

citations: Hagerty, B.B. (2016). Life Reimagined
Jung, C.G. (1933). Modern Man in Search of a Soul
Brown, B. (2010). Midlife unraveling (blog post/talk)

leadingExperts.name: Dr. Toni Bernhard
leadingExperts.contribution: Chronic illness and Buddhism; radical acceptance

leadingExperts.name: Dr. Gabor Maté
leadingExperts.contribution: Stress-illness connection; When the Body Says No

keyFindings: Chronic illness involves grief - mourning former self/life
Acceptance (not resignation) correlates with better outcomes
Social support crucial but often declines over time
Mind-body connection significant in chronic conditions

spoonTheory.creator: Christine Miserandino
spoonTheory.concept: Limited energy units ('spoons') that must be budgeted
spoonTheory.application: Helps communicate invisible illness limitations to others

citations: Bernhard, T. (2010). How to Be Sick
Schubiner, H. (2016). Unlearn Your Pain
Maté, G. (2003). When the Body Says No

leadingExperts.name: Dale Carnegie
leadingExperts.contribution: How to Win Friends and Influence People (foundational)

keyFindings: Social intelligence can be learned and developed
Listening is more influential than talking
Mirroring and validation build rapport automatically
Authenticity > technique in long run
citations: Goleman, D. (2006). Social Intelligence
Lieberman, M.D. (2013). Social: Why Our Brains Are Wired to Connect

leadingExperts.name: Logan Ury
leadingExperts.contribution: How to Not Die Alone; behavioral science of dating

keyFindings: Attachment style predicts dating patterns and partner selection
Chemistry ≠ compatibility - attraction can mislead
Maximizers (seeking 'best') less satisfied than satisficers
Vulnerability accelerates connection (36 Questions study)
datingMistakes: Focusing on resume traits (job, looks) vs. experiential traits (kindness, curiosity)
The Spark myth - chemistry doesn't predict relationship success
Settling vs. Exploring - dating paralysis from too many options
Soulmate myth - relationships are built, not found
citations: Fisher, H. (2009). Why Him? Why Her?
Ury, L. (2021). How to Not Die Alone
Tashiro, T. (2014). The Science of Happily Ever After
